(I haven't looked at feedback from others - so some may have already been covered by others - but i wanted to call them out so that we know which ones we feel the same about)

Things i like:
- Module level import is neat. I feel it could address the confusion around "when should i put things in __init__ and when not".
- Resources at the top-level. I found myself wanting top-level resource a lot more often as I'm writing analytical pipelines that share resources, esp when we are using a db as the single source of truth I feel top-level resources can save a lot of typing and make collab easier because configs can be shared by default.
- Typed API is amazing.

Things i have concerns about:
- Production education concerns. Would like to get  @slopp's to weight in.
  * Calling `definitions(..)` in `__init__.py` will need education. For example, just by looking at the code, I'm not sure when the definitions will be loaded.
  * Although I personally like the module-level function call, it may cause onboarding issue for users who are not very familiar with python, as in they may not understand the ` __init__.py` files and ignore it entirely.

Questions
- How would one do `RepositoryDefinition.load_asset_value` in the new world?
- v1.1 or any minor versions (by minor i mean 1.x) feels a right time to introduce changes like this. should we get this in v1.1 so we can get feedback earlier?
- Similar question^ how do we plan to roll this out? Although it's a non-invasive change, it'd involve a lot of docs/examples changes, which I'd also consider as breaking changes to content.

My main feedback here is that `register_definitions` seems fairly magical. As we briefly discussed, I think it would be easy for a user to guess that it writes the definitions to some sort of cloud service.

IMO the least magical thing would be to communicate to the user that they're setting a module-scoped variable.  Either with a name like `assign_definitions_to_module`. Or a reserved attr called something like `defs` and then they do
```
defs = definitions(...)
```

Thoughts?

Hi @emlys , this solution looks great to me! Thanks for thinking hard about the float edge case. It's definitely something we can put in the issue queue if we want to. It didn't sink in until after our call that the case you might be most worried about is if someone *thinks* they have integer-like values (e.g. 3.0) but they end up on the wrong side of a `numpy.digitize` bin. I was more fixated on the fact that no one would reasonably ask to reclassify a number like 3.00000000001, so we shouldn't design for it. 

Anyway I think it's good to get this clear fix in like you have it, and deal with the rest another time if needed.

I glanced at the tests we already have for `reclassify_raster` and I think they're adequate to cover these changes. I noticed we actually do test with a float raster, which seems appropriate. 

One minor change requested, see my note about the Exception class.

Nice. Could you please copy those syntax examples from the design doc into the PR description? I think it will help future people who `git blame` to understand this change.

Also, I'm not following why parameters come before the generated part? Is that for parity with URLs, like Benjy mentioned? I'm still trying to reason through how target generators will interact with parametrization, e.g. if `overrides` is legal:

```python
python_sources(
   overrides={"utils.py": {"resolves": parametrize("a", "b")}},
)
```

I hope that is, `overrides` is important for our goal of precise metadata. 

This example speaks to why my intuition would think the parameters come _after_ the generated target. We go from least specific to most specific. The parameters are variants of ~the same target, they get more specific.

--

In a followup, you'll also need to update our specs code: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/12914

It is an interesting proposition, quite simple, but it has some flaws. In particular, the association of these two flags will not work on a `certonly` command, since the `certonly` command in dry run mode does not write any certificate on the filesystem, so the deploy hooks will fail.

More generally testing the certbot issuance process itself with a dry run, and testing the deploy hooks logic, are two different concepts. This is a part of my argumentation in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5658. @bmw also suggested a design proposition that follows that approach in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5658#issuecomment-494570998, and also proposed to make a +1 vote on the general design of this functionality before starting the implementation.

This is problematic:

1. obviously, you forgot the other branch of the conditional (with profiling enabled, there's also a `processor.process()`...)
2. your cached processors were all constructed with a non-null `workspace` argument, which causes them to `chdir` into that:
https://github.com/OCR-D/core/blob/9ae6ddfc8798a07bced98e1d1c110e514c058bfd/ocrd/ocrd/processor/base.py#L140-L142
3. your current `workspace.directory` might not be an absolute path; so it would become relative to the previous workspace, not to CWD of the caller

So either you rebase to the `processor.old_pwd`, or pass `workspace=None` when caching, or resolve to absolute here before calling.

(BTW, in my [PR](https://github.com/OCR-D/core/pull/652) I also moved the chdir to `run_api` alone, which would be the equivalent of the second option I mentioned.)

You need to exercise **minimalism**. What I would like you to do (please ask for help if you run into any difficulty at any step):

- [ ] Read my comments below, but don't follow up on them yet. Just make some mental notes.

> - We could potentially add some public API on the application so that it's easier to premptively resolve the request routing? Ideally we ensure that can be cached, so that the lookup only occurs once.
> - We could add the ability to have some kinds of middleware run after the request routing. (Probably not super keen on that.)

```python
app = Starlette(
    middleware=[
        Middleware(PotatoMiddleware, path_prefix="/potato")  # or `path_prefixes`
    ]
)
```

Considering the above, we stick with the middleware concept being only at the app level.

I can't overstress how much I dislike bare asserts and how I don't want them in music21.   (I hate them more than mypy hates hasattr).   In nearly every case where they appear, I would prefer to have type checking disabled on the parent function (return "Any") than have users need to deal with asserting that things not supported in this version of Python (like Literal[False]) don't apply.  

Maybe we can put this into a m21 v8 branch, which won't support Python 3.7, and that will have fewer cases of teaching the system about something explained in the docs, but here I count one possible bug that mypy found, compared to at least 9 workarounds for bugs/deficiencies in their own system.

Mypy conformance might have been something easy to get in if music21 were being started from scratch in 2022, but at this point there are so much code history that cannot cleanly be brought into line with what mypy wants that I'm not sure it's going to be possible to get everything into the typechecking system until they become more flexible on what they're able to read.

@vfdev-5 Thanks a lot for the investigations and for sending the PR.

We had long discussions with Victor about this because it's a tough call but I'll summarize here the reasons why we believe we should merge this BC-breaking change. After looking into the original implementations of AutoAugment and RandAugment, it became clear that their Shear method was using the top-left as the center. Unfortunately TorchVision didn't support this option until it was raised by the author of TrivialAugment @SamuelGabriel at #5204. This means that our implementation deviated from the canonical and thus we consider this discrepancy a bug.

Though I don't believe that this discrepancy had major effect on the overall accuracy of the models that we trained previously, I think it's worth aligning TorchVision's behaviour with the rest of the ecosystem as the majority of frameworks and libraries we checked all implement the Shear operator with center the top-left corner.

I recommend leaving this PR unmerged for a couple of days to allow for people to comment. If there are no objections we can merge it early next week.

In a codeowners file, the last matching pattern takes precedence (see the [docs](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/about-code-owners#example-of-a-codeowners-file)), by putting the catch-all at the bottom of the file, it will override any previous entries. Please move it to the top. (This is why github UI says the CODEOWNERS file is currently invalid)

That said, why do you want to be the default codeowner? As @PerchunPak pointed out, you'll be requested to review every PR automatically. Not only that, due our branch protection rules, no PR will even be mergable until you submit an approving review.

This will almost certainly end up slowing down merging a lot, especially when it comes to smaller PRs, where a review from any of the maintainers (me/PerchunPak) would suffice. When you're the default codeowner, that wouldn't be the case, and your review would become a requirement for merging even just a typo fix / dependabot PRs.

Do you really want to have to review every single new PR?

I had a look through and I think the only thing I can think that needs to change in this PR is making a hard-coded packetsize constant `static constexpr`. Otherwise, the following set of general changes is implemented: 

- `stream_base::queue_storage` changes from `storage_type` to `queue_entry`.
- `spead2::detail::storage` is a vendored `std::aligned_storage` with a cleaner API.
- `std::make_unique` used instead of `std::unique_ptr<T>(new T)`
- `std::launder` used to avoid unwanted compiler optimisations on placement new allocated types.
- Removes some callable wrapper classes in favour of c++14 lambdas
- `[[maybe_unused]]` to remove compiler warnings for unused arguments. In other places, arguments are anonymised. I wondered why there is a distinction between the two cases? Possibly for the sake of existing API calls?

This brings back memories from this deep debugging session leading to a Werkzeug bug report!

I don't have any big objection to the changes (except maybe the question about `_more_body`), and the `BufferedReader` will take care of optimising the `RawIOBase` generic implementations. Could it actually be faster in the end, as the buffer will be a `memoryview`? Reading the [implementation](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/main/Lib/_pyio.py#L1026) is pretty interesting. 

It really simplifies our implementation, and it feels nice to be able to use python built-ins.

I might have a stupid question: if I understood well, the flow is the following, starting from zero:
- we try to get data from the `ASGIReceiveCallable`
- we get data, but more than what the buffer (from the `BufferedReader` in that case) might be able to take
- we save the whole data internally, even what we have written
- next call, if we have data saved, we don't even try to receive from the coroutine but just return what we had saved, which might be 1 byte even if the buffer can take way more.
- bad cases: next call, we actually might hit the end of the coroutine, and will just return 0. Or we just had 10 bytes that we could have fit in the previous call.
- kinda good case, we have just under the size of the buffer, and doing anything above would not have helped. Or we still have way more than the buffer can take, and continue.

I understand that it might complicate the implementation a lot, or that it shouldn't be at all what we should do 😅
I'm also not sure what limits we could have to not take too much from the coroutine, because we might buffer way too much into the raw stream, but I'd try to get data if I have space in the buffer (which we can know if the `_buffered_body` is not `None`).
I also don't have an idea in what cases we fall most often. Do we often get a big body from the coroutine, and a small buffer from the `BufferedReader`, which would make sense to drain with multiple calls? Or are we more often with a big buffer able to eat the whole body?
The buffer into a buffer is playing games with me. 😅

Anyway, love that we're using more primitives like this! 😄

After a little bit of debugging, looks like the error stems from a None value for a header being sent [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/9ad85bb9675f08972796fe32fff08eb7b468da2c/tools/azure-sdk-tools/devtools_testutils/sanitizers.py#L561) where the headers are `{'x-abstraction-identifier': 'CustomDefaultMatcher', 'x-recording-id': None}`. Seems like `requests`  drops the `None` header at some point which is why the request works with `requests`, but this `None` value is causing issues when using urllib3 directly. 

Should we ensure that a recording_id is set so that the header is not None (or just not send this header if the recording_id is None)?

This feels like an okay fix for now to address the common user-facing issue - but I will say I think we will need to directionally do one of two things:
1. Ensure that we validate all strings that we convert to type tokens at the point we take them from the outside world, and produce useful error messages there - and then assume that all things typed as `token.Type` are valid
2. Admit that we aren't cleanly ensuring that all things typed as `token.Type` are valid, and change the API for `token.Type` to return errors instead of asserting (which would require a *lot* of changes to consumers to handle all the new error cases.

I personally think we should be doing (1).  The changes in this PR aren't really either (1) or (2) - they are patching the problem in between these two in one place (but I'll bet there are dozens more like this as latent issues).

Just a few comments after going through the inclusion process. In short, I **was** successful in adding a device!

I was surprised that when I clicked on the "Add Device" button it already started the inclusion process with the Default strategy. Being used to zwavejs2mqtt's UI and also the previous implementation of this dialog, it was a bit strange. Not that I'm against it. It does however feel unbalanced since the "Remove Device" dialog still has an explicit start action. I do like how it defaults to the "Default" strategy w/o prompting, and lets you change it.

After triggering my S2-supporting device, the UI didn't show me the dialog to grant security classes as it is supposed to. Instead, it seemed to have sent over the requested classes to the server w/o prompting me, and went straight to the PIN entry. So for some reason that dialog was skipped.

The final dialog that tells me the process was completed isn't clear about whether the inclusion was successful or not with respect to Security. It doesn't mention whether the inclusion was with low or high security, the latter which should show kind of warning.

The Device page doesn't show what kind of security the node was added with, so besides the missing inclusion completion dialog, it's not possible to verify afterwards whether it was included with S2 security or not. (I think this capability doesn't exist yet in the python lib)

I think this is fine. The consequence of not being able to read the file is very small: if a worker administrator has set up a worker to run a limited number of tasks (e.g. after 10 tasks, don't run anymore) then the counter would be reset. However, this is quite a contrived setup, and probably this has never been done.

The `numberOfTasksToRun` config setting was added when we had the simple engine, so that a machine administrator could guarantee that some event occurred after no more than X tasks had run (e.g. a puppet run), by having generic-worker exit after X tasks. Then when multiuser engine was created, the running count was persisted to disk in order to be functionally similar despite the reboots.

In reality, if there are reboots, administrators would probably run their external task (such as a puppet run) on machine bootup, before generic-worker is started, so it isn't really useful.

When multiuser no longer needs to reboot between tasks for non-UI tasks, and this becomes more important again, the task count will be in memory already, so won't need to persist that state to disk, so then the problem also goes away.

So in other words, this is all good. Another option is that we just don't support the feature when there are reboots, and don't write the file to disk, but we can do that later if there are further problems after this one is solved.

Thank you for the contribution!

In the previous implementation, the instance `_MultiWorkerIter` will be created after the function `__iter__` is called. It takes some time to load the first `prefetch` batch data.
```python
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset)  # create a worker pool, but no task to do
for x in dataloader:  # `__iter__` is called, and the first batch is loading.
    # wait the loading of the first batch in the first iteration
    y = model(x)
```

In this PR, the first batch of next epoch is prefetched. The performance is boosted.
```python
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset)  # create a worker pool, and prefetch the first batch
for x in dataloader:
    # the first batch of the next epoch is prefetching.
    y = model(x)
```

However, users may change the state of the Dataset, for example:
```python
dataset = create_dataset()
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset)
for epoch in range(start_epoch, end_epoch):
    for x in dataloader:
        y = model(x)
    dataset.set_epoch(epoch)  # change the state of dataset
```

It will break the logic. The dataloader will return a batch with old state in the next epoch. 

The another problem is that he occupancy of shared memory and GPU memory will be increased, because of the extra MultiWorkerIter.

A new DataLoader argument is good to decide whether to prefetch the first batch of next epoch. : )

I provide explanations to some modifications below:

One concern I have is that the `MultiProcessDataLoader` is only being used to load `Instances`, and so we lose out on one of it's advantages: that indexing, padding, and batching can be done in worker processes, not the main process.

But by only using the `MultiProcessDataLoader` to call `iter_instances()`, `MultiTaskDataLoader` becomes responsible for indexing, padding, and batching, which then have to be done in the main process unless we add some extra multi-processing logic to `MultiTaskDataLoader`, which I think is a bad idea because that stuff is complicated.

Here's an alternative suggestion:

`MultiTaskDataLoader` becomes a very thin wrapper around `MultiProcessDataLoader` that just uses a special `BatchSampler` to handle the scheduling and make sure only instances from the same task are batched together.

I'm pretty sure this would work, but I haven't thought through every detail. I also realize it's probably unrealistic to make big changes this late in the game. I don't think this would actually require any API changes though, so we might be able to make these changes later on if it's worth it without breaking backwards compatibility.

I think the inheritance here is problematic. By having the Async classes inherit from the sync ones, we're breaking LSP ([Liskov Substitution Principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liskov_substitution_principle)), and it's also not following the base `MCServer` class, which specifies that the `status` method needs to be synchronous.

If we wanted to do this properly, we'd need a `SyncMCServer` and `AsyncMCServer` classes, which `SyncJavaServer`, `AsyncJavaServer`, `SyncBedrockServer` and `AsyncBedrockServer` whould need to inherit from. Alternatively, we could have `MCServer` base that only defines shared things across both sync and async versions, and let the individual classes inherit from that.

As much as I'd like to do this, I'm not sure I like this kind of structure, and the proper structure (above) would be kind of big and annoying to deal with. I'm not entirely sure that this is a good change to make, next time it may be a good idea to make an issue before making a PR, so that we can discuss whether this kind of change will even make sense.

I would however like to hear @kevinkjt2000's opinion here, I'm not completely against doing this, but not if it means breaking LSP and the base `MCServer` not actually being correct type-wise with the async classes.
